topic: Brazilian food craving — trying to make pao de queijo from scratch

turns.content: okay I need help URGENTLY haha. I am in the middle of trying to make pao de queijo from scratch and things are not going well
I'm ready to help with the pao de queijo rescue mission! What's happening right now — is the dough too sticky, too dry, not puffing up? Walk me through what stage you're at.

so I followed a recipe I found online and the dough is like... super sticky? like impossibly sticky. I can't even form the little balls, it just sticks to everything. I already added the tapioca flour and the eggs and the cheese but gente it's a disaster. My hands are covered, the counter is covered, ai meu Deus

That level of stickiness is actually pretty common with pao de queijo dough, especially if the liquid was still very hot when you added the eggs. A few things to try right now: wet your hands with a little oil (any neutral oil) before shaping the balls. The dough is supposed to be quite soft and a bit sticky — it's not like bread dough. Also, try refrigerating the dough for 15-20 minutes. Cold dough firms up and becomes way easier to handle. Did you use polvilho azedo (sour tapioca starch) or regular tapioca flour?

this is the problem!! I couldn't find polvilho azedo anywhere here so I used regular tapioca starch. I went to like three supermarkets and a Brazilian shop that didn't have it in stock. You think that's the issue?

That's definitely a factor. Polvilho azedo is fermented tapioca starch, and the fermentation gives it different properties — it makes the dough stretchier and gives pao de queijo that characteristic chewy, slightly tangy interior and the crispy outside. Regular tapioca starch works but the dough behaves differently: it tends to be stickier and less elastic. You can still make it work though. The oil-on-hands trick plus chilling the dough should help a lot. Some people also mix in a small amount of regular wheat flour — maybe a tablespoon or two — to give the dough more structure when using regular tapioca starch.

okay okay I'm putting it in the fridge right now. honestly this is making me miss Brazil so much because you can literally buy a bag of pao de queijo mix at ANY supermarket and it comes out perfect every time. Or you just go to any bakery on any corner and they have fresh ones haha. Here I'm fighting with tapioca starch like it's my enemy

I can imagine the frustration — going from having perfect pao de queijo on every corner to wrestling with substitute ingredients in a kitchen far from home must be rough. But honestly, the fact that you're trying to make it from scratch is pretty great. Once you crack the technique with the ingredients you can find locally, you'll be able to make them whenever the craving hits. Have you tried checking if there's a Brazilian community group online where people might share supplier tips for hard-to-find ingredients?

there is actually! There's a WhatsApp group for Brazilians here and someone mentioned a shop that imports polvilho azedo but it's kind of far from where I live. I need to make the trip because this regular tapioca starch situation is not it haha. Okay the dough has been in the fridge for a bit, let me try again... oh! OH it's actually way better now! I can form the balls!

There you go! Cold dough for the win. Make the balls roughly golf-ball sized, place them on a baking sheet with some space between them, and bake at around 190-200 degrees Celsius. They should puff up nicely and get golden on the outside — takes about 20-25 minutes usually. Don't open the oven door in the first 15 minutes or they might deflate.

got it got it, hands oiled, balls formed, they look a little uneven but honestly I don't care at this point haha. Going in the oven now. Nossa I really hope these come out okay because if they don't I'm going to be so sad. There is nothing like biting into a warm pao de queijo querida, nothing in this world

Uneven is totally fine — homemade ones always look a bit rustic and that's part of the charm. The real test is the inside: if it's soft, stretchy, and cheesy, you've nailed it even if they're not bakery-pretty. Set a timer for 20 minutes and resist the urge to peek. I hope they're delicious — you earned these after all that dough wrestling!

hahaha dough wrestling is exactly what happened here. Okay 20 minutes, I'm setting the timer. Thank you so much for the help meu bem!! Next time I'm getting the real polvilho azedo even if I have to drive across the city for it

That's the spirit — real polvilho azedo will make a huge difference next time. Enjoy the pao de queijo when they come out, and don't forget to let them cool for just a minute or two before biting in. Hot cheese burns are no joke! Good luck!

UPDATE — they came out!! They're not pretty but gente they taste AMAZING. The outside is crispy and the inside is all gooey and cheesy. I'm literally eating them standing in the kitchen right now, burning my tongue because I couldn't wait haha. Okay they're a TINY bit flat compared to the real ones back home but honestly? I don't even care. I made pao de queijo from scratch in the middle of the desert and I'm so proud of myself!!

That's a win! Crispy outside, gooey inside — that's the hallmark of a good pao de queijo, even if they're a little flat. The flatness is probably the tapioca starch substitution; with the real polvilho azedo they'll puff up more next time. Enjoy every single one — you earned them after that kitchen battle.
